rules.name: narrow_solution
rules.description: Code is hardcoded to a specific type, format, or use case when a general solution is feasible.
rules.detection: Check if function parameters use concrete types where generics/templates/protocols could be used. Check if switch/if chains handle only one case when the domain has many.
rules.fix: Parameterise the type. Use generics, templates, interfaces, or union types as appropriate for the language.

rules.name: symptom_patching
rules.description: A bug fix modifies a leaf module when the root cause is in a shared caller or base class.
rules.detection: Compare the proposed change location against the call chain. If the change is at the end and the problem suggests broader impact, flag it.
rules.fix: Trace the call chain upward. Identify the shared point where the bug originates. Fix at that level.
rules.bad_example: Fixing RedstoneHandler.activate() when UseItem.execute() passes wrong context to all handlers.
rules.good_example: Fixing UseItem.execute() to pass correct interaction context, resolving issues for all item types.

rules.name: exception_swallowing
rules.description: A catch/except block catches an exception and does nothing, only logs, or returns a default without recovery.
rules.detection: Identify catch blocks with empty bodies, log-only bodies, or return null/default without context.
rules.fix: Either handle the exception meaningfully (retry, fallback) or let it propagate. If logging, also re-throw.
rules.good_example: try { ... } catch (e) { logger.error('Context:', e); throw new AppError('Operation failed', { cause: e }); }

rules.name: hallucinated_api
rules.description: Code references a function, method, class, or module that does not exist in the target language or version.
rules.detection: Cross-reference API calls against known standard library surfaces for the target version.
rules.fix: Use the correct API for the target version, or implement the missing functionality manually.
rules.bad_example: std::ranges::to<vector>(range) // Does not exist in C++20
rules.good_example: std::vector<int> v(range.begin(), range.end()); // Works in C++20

rules.name: copy_paste_duplication
rules.description: The same logic appears in two or more places with minor variations.
rules.detection: Identify repeated code blocks with >70% structural similarity.
rules.fix: Extract a shared function parameterised by the differences.
rules.bad_example:
function processUserOrder() { validate(); save(); notify(); }
function processAdminOrder() { validate(); save(); notify(); }
rules.good_example: function processOrder(role: Role) { validate(); save(); notify(role); }

rules.name: hardcoded_configuration
rules.description: Magic numbers, hardcoded file paths, embedded credentials, or environment-specific values in source code.
rules.detection: Identify literal values that should be configuration: paths, URLs, port numbers, credentials, thresholds.
rules.fix: Extract to configuration (environment variables, config file, named constants).

rules.name: missing_error_paths
rules.description: Code only handles the happy path with no input validation or failure handling.
rules.detection: Check function entry points for input validation. Check external calls for error handling.
rules.fix: Add input validation at function entry. Add error handling around all external calls.
rules.bad_example: function divide(a, b) { return a / b; }
rules.good_example: function divide(a: number, b: number): number { if (b === 0) throw new Error('Division by zero'); return a / b; }

rules.name: untestable_structure
rules.description: Code cannot be unit tested without modification due to hidden deps, static calls, or constructor side effects.
rules.detection: Identify direct instantiation of service dependencies inside business logic. Identify static I/O calls. Identify constructor side effects.
rules.fix: Inject dependencies via constructor or method parameters. Wrap static calls behind interfaces.
rules.bad_example: class OrderService { process() { const db = new Database(); db.save(this.order); } }
rules.good_example: class OrderService { constructor(private db: IDatabase) {} process() { this.db.save(this.order); } }

rules.name: over_engineering
rules.description: Code applies patterns, abstractions, or indirection that current requirements do not justify.
rules.detection: Count layers of indirection relative to concrete implementations. Flag patterns with only one implementation.
rules.fix: Simplify. Apply patterns when there's a concrete need, not speculatively.
rules.bad_example: AbstractFactoryBuilderStrategyProvider for creating one simple config object.

rules.name: dependency_bloat
rules.description: A large library is imported for trivial functionality.
rules.detection: Cross-reference imported packages against usage. Flag where <3 exports are used and functionality is trivially implementable.
rules.fix: Implement the needed functionality directly, or use a smaller focused package.
rules.good_example: const get = (obj, path) => path.split('.').reduce((o, k) => o?.[k], obj);

rules.name: ignoring_language_conventions
rules.description: Code written in language B using patterns from language A.
rules.detection: Pattern match against known cross-language anti-patterns per language pair.
rules.fix: Rewrite using the target language's idiomatic approach.
rules.bad_example:
# Python with Java-style getters
def getName(self): return self._name

rules.name: repetitive_boilerplate
rules.description: Large amounts of repetitive boilerplate that could be eliminated with language features.
rules.detection: Identify structurally identical blocks differing only in names or types.
rules.fix: Use the language's abstraction mechanisms (generics, macros, decorators, metaprogramming).
rules.good_example: function parse<T>(v: unknown, parser: Parser<T>): T { ... }

rules.name: untestable_structure
rules.description: Code cannot be unit tested without modification due to hidden deps, static calls, or constructor side effects.
rules.detection: Identify deeply chained static calls, new in business logic, or hardcoded file paths.
rules.fix: Inject dependencies via constructor or method parameters. Wrap static calls behind interfaces.

rules.name: magic_numbers
rules.description: Raw numeric literals used in logic without explanation. Makes code harder to understand and maintain.
rules.detection: Detect numeric literals > 1 used in conditionals, timeouts, array indices, or return values.
rules.fix: Extract to named constants: const MAX_RETRIES = 3; const TIMEOUT_MS = 5000;

rules.name: missing_return_type
rules.description: Functions without explicit return types rely on type inference, which can silently change when the implementation changes.
rules.detection: Detect exported or public functions without return type annotations.
rules.fix: Add explicit return types to all exported/public functions.
